title: The Virgin Mary, Prophet Joel, St. Keladion of Alexandria & St. Zacharias of Jerusalem
narrative:
The Ever-Virgin Mary Theotokos, the Prophet Joel, St. Keladion Pope of Alexandria, and St. Zacharias Patriarch of Jerusalem

On the twenty-first of Yekatit, the monthly commemoration of the most holy Theotokos gathers around it three additional witnesses whose lives illuminate different aspects of the Church's waiting, watching, and faithful preservation of the sacred trust. The Virgin Mary, Ever-Virgin and Mother of God, is honoured on this day with the full solemnity that the Ethiopian liturgical tradition brings to her monthly feast — the Weddase Maryam is sung, the Qurbana is offered, and the faithful pour out their needs before her who carried in her womb the salvation of the world. The monthly Marian feast is not merely a devotional observance but a theological act: the Church affirming, month by month, that the Incarnation is not a past event but a present reality, that the one born of Mary is still among His people, and that His Mother's intercession is still operative and powerful.

The Prophet Joel stands alongside the Theotokos as the voice that proclaimed, centuries before Pentecost, the outpouring of the Spirit that the Incarnation would make possible: I will pour out my Spirit upon all flesh, and your sons and your daughters shall prophesy. The Ethiopian tradition reads Joel's prophecy as directly fulfilled in Mary — she is the first and fullest instance of the Spirit's outpouring, the one upon whom the Holy Spirit came in the most intimate and decisive way possible, overshadowing her so that the Holy One born of her should be called the Son of God. Joel dreamed of what Mary experienced, and his dream is honoured alongside her reality on this day.

Saint Keladion, the eleventh Pope of Alexandria in the succession of Saint Mark, is this day's figure of institutional faithfulness — a patriarch who governed the ancient see with the quietness and diligence that the tradition associates with those whose greatness lies in the quality of their stewardship rather than in dramatic individual acts. Keladion received the apostolic tradition that had been handed down from Mark through nine predecessors and passed it on intact to his successor, and this transmission — faithful, unspectacular, essential — is the form of sanctity that every functioning institution depends upon but rarely celebrates. The Ethiopian Church, which traces its own patriarchal succession through Alexandria, honours Keladion as one of the indispensable links in a chain that reaches from the apostle to the present day.

Saint Zacharias, Patriarch of Jerusalem, is the figure of suffering preservation that this day most urgently commends to the faithful's attention. When the Persian army of Khosrow II sacked Jerusalem in 614, Zacharias was carried into captivity in Persia along with thousands of Christian prisoners and the most precious relic of the holy city — the True Cross. He spent fourteen years in Persian captivity, tending his imprisoned flock, maintaining the liturgical life of the community as best he could in the conditions of captivity, and negotiating for the welfare of his people with the Persian authorities. When Heraclius finally defeated Persia and recovered the Cross, Zacharias was among those ransomed, and he returned to Jerusalem to see the relic restored to the Church of the Resurrection. The tradition honours him as a shepherd who stayed with his flock in captivity as he had in freedom, who made the liturgy survive in conditions that seemed designed to destroy it, and who trusted that the Cross, like its Lord, would rise again. Amen.
